4. Compute missing_ab: sum over runs of only 'c's, for each run of length l, add l*(l+1)/2.

6. invalid = (missing_a + missing_b + missing_c) - (missing_ab + missing_ac + missing_bc).

7. valid = total - invalid.

int count_substrings(const string& s, const string& allowed) {
    int n = s.size();
    int res = 0;
    int current_run = 0;
    for (int i=0; i<n; ++i) {
        if (allowed.find(s[i]) != string::npos) {
            current_run++;
        } else {
            res += current_run * (current_run + 1) / 2;
            current_run = 0;
        }
    }
    res += current_run * (current_run + 1) / 2;
    return res;
}

So, in code:

int numberOfSubstrings(string s) {
    int n = s.size();
    int total = n * (n + 1) / 2;

    auto count_substrings = [](const string& s, const string& allowed) {
        int res = 0;
        int current_run = 0;
        for (char c : s) {
            if (allowed.find(c) != string::npos) {
                current_run++;
            } else {
                res += current_run * (current_run + 1) / 2;
                current_run = 0;
            }
        }
        res += current_run * (current_run + 1) / 2;
        return res;
    };

    int missing_a = count_substrings(s, "bc");
    int missing_b = count_substrings(s, "ac");
    int missing_c = count_substrings(s, "ab");

    int missing_ab = count_substrings(s, "c");
    int missing_ac = count_substrings(s, "b");
    int missing_bc = count_substrings(s, "a");

    int invalid = (missing_a + missing_b + missing_c) - (missing_ab + missing_ac + missing_bc);
    return total - invalid;
}

Wait, but in the helper function, allowed is the set of allowed characters. So for missing_a, the allowed are 'b' and 'c', so the substrings can consist of any combination of 'b' and 'c', but not 'a'.

Yes, that's correct.

Testing this code with the examples should give the correct results.

Now, considering the constraints, this approach is O(n) time, which is efficient for n up to 5e4.

So, the code should work.

solution:
```cpp
#include <string>

using namespace std;

class Solution {
public:
    int numberOfSubstrings(string s) {
        int n = s.size();
        int total = n * (n + 1) / 2;

        auto count_substrings = [](const string& s, const string& allowed) {
            int res = 0;
            int current_run = 0;
            for (char c : s) {
                if (allowed.find(c) != string::npos) {
                    current_run++;
                } else {
                    res += current_run * (current_run + 1) / 2;
                    current_run = 0;
                }
            }
            res += current_run * (current_run + 1) / 2;
            return res;
        };

        int missing_a = count_substrings(s, "bc");
        int missing_b = count_substrings(s, "ac");
        int missing_c = count_substrings(s, "ab");

        int missing_ab = count_substrings(s, "c");
        int missing_ac = count_substrings(s, "b");
        int missing_bc = count_substrings(s, "a");

        int invalid = (missing_a + missing_b + missing_c) - (missing_ab + missing_ac + missing_bc);
        return total - invalid;
    }
};
```

efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n)
space complexity: O(1)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution efficiently calculates the number of valid substrings by using the inclusion-exclusion principle. It counts all possible substrings and subtracts those that do not contain all three required characters, achieving linear time complexity.
